title: failed to reexec: Permission denied
body:
### Issue Description

Hello everyone I have built the podman from source on my ubuntu 24.04 on ec2 instance, everything looks good I can pull the images I can run the container, I can build the images. but each time when i reboot the instance or want to run the podman for the first time I get this error 
failed to reexec: Permission denied
but when I repeat the command again it works:

podman version

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.3.0
API Version:  5.3.0
Go Version:   go1.22.6
Git Commit:   874bf2c301ecf0ba645f1bb45f81966cc755b7da
Built:        Thu Nov 21 16:21:39 2024
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```
any idea? 
thanks for your help

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. run any podman commands for the first time



### Describe the results you received

failed to reexec: Permission denied


### Describe the results you expected

return normal output

comments.body: Probably related to the rootless pause process, which we both create and try to join on first run, then only join on subsequent runs. You can do a `podman system migrate` and if that causes the error again that confirms the cause.

Hi @mheon Thanks for your answer, even for running this command 
podman system migrate I get the same error for the first time, and after the second time and rebooting the system, nothing has changed.
BTW, there is no container running on the system

Do you apparmor running, if so can you see if it logs anything or try to disable it.
The error here is just from a simple `execvp ("/proc/self/exe", argv);` so that should not be blocked normally.

Hi @Luap99  Thanks for you message after disabling the apparmor and rebooting the system,
now I can not running the podman at all :D 

```
cannot clone: Permission denied
Error: cannot re-exec process
```

another update, I just fully uninstalled apparmor from the system and now I don't get that error anymore on any podman commands 
but another error is that when I run the following command: 

podman run -d nginx

I get this error 

```
Error: loading seccomp profile () failed: seccomp not enabled in this build
```
but I built the podman without any BUILDTAGS just like the documentation: 

```
Build Tags
Otherwise, if you do not want to build Podman with seccomp or selinux support you can add BUILDTAGS="" when running make.

make BUILDTAGS=""
sudo make install
```

and from the podman info command
 
```
  security:
    apparmorEnabled: false
    capabilities: CAP_CHOWN,CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE,CAP_FOWNER,CAP_FSETID,CAP_KILL,CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE,CAP_SETFCAP,CAP_SETGID,CAP_SETPCAP,CAP_SETUID,CAP_SYS_CHROOT
    rootless: true
    seccompEnabled: false
    seccompProfilePath: ""
    selinuxEnabled: false

```

There really isn't a reason to to disable seccomp. I think maybe you need to use `--security-opt seccomp=unconfined` if you don't want it.
But running without seccomp is not something we recommend, I would say we should fix the build docs. I don't even know why we allow compiling without seccomp support.

> cannot clone: Permission denied

That means we cannot create the user namespace which almost certainly is blokced by the new apparmor restrictions on ubuntu or some other seccomp profile when run in a nested container for example.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I ran into the same issue, and found that AppArmor was denying an execution of `/proc/self/exec`:

```
$ sudo dmesg -t
audit: type=1400 audit(1736291136.943:128): apparmor="DENIED" operation="exec" class="file" info="Failed name lookup - disconnected path" error=-13 profile="unprivileged_userns" name="/proc/self/exe" pid=41842 comm="podman" requested_mask="x" denied_mask="x" fsuid=1000 ouid=0
```

So I added the following to **/etc/apparmor.d/local/podman**:
```
owner /proc/self/exe ix,
audit deny /proc/self/exe rw,
```
The first line allows the current owner of podman to run `/proc/self/exe` only if it is also owned by the same user, and inherit the AppArmor profile. The second line denies read and write access to `/proc/self/exe`, might not be needed since AppArmor denies by default but feels nice to be explicit.

And then verified the AppArmor profile can be parsed correctly with:
```
sudo apparmor_parser -r /etc/apparmor.d/podman
```

And all works well 👍

After some more investigation, it seems that I spoke too soon with the AppArmor profile change suggested above. It turns out it didn't quite solve the issue.

The [podman profile](https://gitlab.com/apparmor/apparmor/-/blob/master/profiles/apparmor.d/podman?ref_type=heads) that is provided by the AppArmor project is supposed to add an exception for podman with a default allow profile via the `unconfined` flag ([man apparmor](https://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/noble/man5/apparmor.d.5.html)):

```
# This profile allows everything and only exists to give the
# application a name instead of having the label "unconfined"

abi <abi/4.0>,
include <tunables/global>

profile podman /usr/bin/podman flags=(unconfined) {
  userns,

  # Site-specific additions and overrides. See local/README for details.
  include if exists <local/podman>
}
```

Which is applied and works correctly when installing Podman via apt which installs into `/usr/bin/podman`. But when you compile from source and run `sudo make install` Podman installs into `/usr/local/bin/podman`. And so this AppArmor profile is not applied (because it only applies to `/usr/bin/podman`). AppArmor then denies the execution of `/proc/self/exe` in the newly created restricted user namespace:

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/1a00c92e005a490a6a2389618d9f55465cf73141/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L1100-L1101

So on the one hand, Ubuntu has introduced restricted user namespaces in AppArmor by default. And AppArmor did add an exception for Podman, when installed to `/usr/bin/podman` which is the case via the Ubuntu podman package.

But on the other hand, building Podman from source installs into `/usr/local/bin/podman`, but now in order to support the latest version of Ubuntu a new AppArmor profile could be installed that specifically allows `/usr/local/bin/podman`.

If the AppArmor project were to update the profile they could change:
```
profile podman /usr/{bin,local/bin}/podman flags=(unconfined) {
```
Which would apply the podman profile from both `/usr/bin/podman` and `/usr/local/bin/podman`. I'll suggest this, linking to this post for context.
For now, on Ubuntu 24.04, it seems sufficient to manually edit the podman profile `/etc/apparmor.d/podman` and change the path to `/usr/local/bin/podman`.
